q: A plant disease is usually defined as abnormal growth and/or dysfunction of a plant.
Biotic diseases are caused by living organisms such as fungi, bacteria, and viruses.
Abiotic diseases are caused by living organisms like fungi and nematodes.
Plant disease is any abnormal condition that alters the appearance or function of a plant.
Most plant diseases, around 85 percent, are caused by viral and bacterial organisms.
Nematodes are very tiny roundworms that are plant parasites and help distribute bacteria and fungi through the soil.
Some plant diseases are classified as abiotic, or diseases that are non-infectious and include damage from air pollution.
An infectious agent is capable of reproducing within or on its host and spreading from one susceptible host to another.
Non-infectious causal agents are organisms capable of reproducing within a host.
The study of plant diseases is called plant pathology.
Pathology is derived from the two Greek words pathos (suffering, disease) and logos (discourse, study).
Biotic diseases can spread throughout one plant and also may spread to neighbouring plants of the same species.
Biotic diseases sometimes show physical evidence of the pathogen, such as fungal growth, bacterial ooze, or nematode cysts.
Abiotic diseases are caused by nonliving factors, such as drought stress, sunscald, or freeze injury.
Abiotic diseases are caused by conditions external to the plant, not due to living agents.
Abiotic causal agents can spread from plant to plant.
Abiotic damage often occurs on many plant species, while biotic disease problems are more limited to a certain species.
The fungi that cause tomato leaf blight also cause damage on sweet corn.
Abiotic diseases do not show the presence of disease signs.
Plant diseases result from the impact of three factors: the host plant, the pathogen and the environmental conditions.
Signs of plant disease are physical evidence of the pathogen.
Signs are the actual organisms causing the disease, such as fungal fruiting bodies or bacterial ooze.
Symptoms are visible effects of disease on plants in response to a pathogen.
Any detectable changes in colour, shape, and/or functions of the plant in response to a pathogen is a symptom.
Plant symptoms include blight, canker, chlorosis, decline, and dieback.
Viruses cause mottling, spots, mosaic-like patterns, crinkling, and other malformations on leaves and fruits.
Because viruses are systemic, infected plants must be rogued or discarded.
Pathogens can spread from plant to plant and may infect all types of plant tissues including leaves, shoots, stems, crown, roots, tubers, fruits, seeds and vascular tissues.
Fungi and fungal-like organisms (FLOs) cause more plant diseases than any other group of plant pest.
Fungal-like organisms (FLOs) include organisms like Pythium and Phytophthora and cause downy mildew.
Individual species of fungi can only parasitize one kind of plant.
Most plant pathogenic bacteria belong to the genera Agrobacterium, Erwinia, Pseudomonas, Streptomyces and Xanthomonas.
Bacteria that cause plant diseases are most active in cold and dry environments.
Bacteria that cause plant diseases can be splashed about by rain or carried by the wind, birds or insects.
Viruses are intracellular (inside cells) pathogenic particles that infect other living organisms.
Unlike bacteria and fungi, viruses are spread by water or wind.
Mosaics are characterized by the formation of light green, yellow, or white spots intermingled with the normal green aerial plant structures.
Ringspots are characterized by the appearance of chlorotic or necrotic rings on the leaves.
Nematodes are simple, multicellular animals and are bilaterally symmetrical, soft-bodied, non-segmented roundworms.
Plant parasitic nematodes have a stylet, a piercing mouthpart, which is the key diagnostic sign differentiating them from other nematodes.
Biological control is control of plant diseases using living microorganisms.
In biological control, an antagonist may secrete metabolites (antibiotics) harmful to the pathogens.
In biological control, an antagonist may compete with the pathogens for nutrients or space.
Physical methods of disease prevention and control are based on the physiological tolerance of disease agents to adverse conditions such as high or low temperature.
Chemicals used to control bacteria are called fungicides.
Chemicals used to control nematodes are called nematicides and may be applied to seeds, foliage, flowers, fruit, or soil.
Drought stress and chemical injury will likely cause damage on several types of plants.
Slime flux or ooze, and spore masses are examples of signs rather than symptoms.
The fungi belonging to the genus Alternaria cause root rot of many plants.
The genus Xanthomonas causes fire blight of pears and apple.
